q: The coaching sample (Sample A — what to avoid) is mainly for:
o: Learning which mistakes to watch for on real calls
Proving a coworker was wrong
Recording customer credit cards

q: The model sample (Sample B — how to answer), when available, is best used to:
o: Memorize one script word-for-word every time
Compare tone, structure, and thoroughness with Sample A
Replace all pricing training

q: Before you give a firm price for rental items, the safest approach is:
o: Verify against current catalog or office-confirmed pricing
Quote from memory if you are pretty sure
Round down so the customer likes you

q: Which set of details should you usually collect before building a quote?
o: Name, event date, location, and items or package needs
Only the customer's first name
Nothing until they pay a deposit

q: If you are unsure of a price while the customer is on the phone, you should:
o: Say you will verify and follow up, or check with the office while they wait if appropriate
Make up a number so you do not lose the sale
Tell them to guess

q: A customer asks for a quick “ballpark.” The best practice is still to:
o: Gather key details and use official pricing sources — not random numbers
Throw out a rough number without notes
Refuse to help until they book

q: If Sample A showed rushing or skipping details, the fix aligned with Sample B is:
o: Slow down, ask clarifying questions, and record what you learn
End the call faster
Avoid writing anything down

q: The model approach usually includes a professional greeting such as:
o: “Thank you for calling Friendly Party Rental — how can I help you today?”
“Yeah, what do you need?”
“Hold on.”

q: Ending a quote call professionally often includes:
o: Confirming next steps (callback, written quote, booking link, etc.)
Hanging up as soon as you say a price
Assuming they understood without checking

q: Why ask about delivery address and setup surface (grass, pavement, etc.)?
o: They affect delivery, setup, safety, and accurate quoting
They never matter for tents or inflatables
Only for weddings

q: Deposit, cancellation, or payment terms should come from:
o: Official company policy and written terms — not improvised rules
Whatever sounds fair in the moment
The lowest competitor you can name

q: You may need a written quote or email follow-up when:
o: The order is complex or the customer wants something in writing
Never — verbal quotes are always enough
Only for orders under $100

q: For inflatables, surface and space questions matter because:
o: Safety and setup requirements depend on them
They are optional small talk
Only the color of the unit matters

q: Compared with a rushed or dismissive call, Sample B’s style emphasizes:
o: Listening, patience, and clear next steps
Talking over the customer
Avoiding follow-up

q: Main customer phone number for Friendly Party Rental is:
Customer service email for follow-up on quotes:
o: customerservice@friendlypartyrental.com
support@randomrentals.com
info@friendlypartyrental.com

q: Callback number and spelling of name/venue should be:
o: Confirmed when needed so you can reach them with the quote
Skipped to save time
Only collected after they pay

q: A good way to explain booking after quoting:
o: Direct them to friendlypartyrental.com/order-by-date or 315-884-1498 as appropriate
Tell them to figure it out online alone
Say booking is not available
